beats.title: Rama's Exile and Early Adventures
beats.summary: This chapter describes the events following Rama's exile: Sita accompanies him; they cross the Ganga, meet Bharadvaja, and settle in Chitrakuta. After Dasaratha's death, Bharata refuses the throne and visits Rama, who gives him his sandals as a token. Rama then enters Dandaka forest, kills Viradha, meets sages, receives divine weapons from Agastya, mutilates Surpanakha, kills Khara and other demons, and finally Ravana, incited by Surpanakha, abducts Sita after killing Jatayu. Rama meets Kabandha, Sabari, Hanuman, and Sugriva.

beats.title: The Alliance with Sugriva and the War with Ravana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Ramayana describes the alliance between Rama and Sugriva, the slaying of Vali, the search for Sita, Hanuman's leap to Lanka, the burning of Lanka, the building of the bridge, the defeat of Ravana, the coronation of Vibhishana, and Rama's return to Ayodhya.

beats.title: The Origin of the Ramayana
beats.summary: Valmiki, after hearing Narada's account of Rama, goes to the Tamasā river with his disciple Bharadvāja. There he witnesses a hunter kill a male krauñca bird, causing its mate to mourn. Overcome with compassion, Valmiki spontaneously utters a curse in metrical form, which becomes the first śloka of the Ramayana. He then contemplates the nature of his utterance and begins composing the epic.

beats.title: Brahma's Command to Valmiki
beats.summary: Brahma appears to Valmiki, instructs him to compose the Ramayana in verse, and assures him that his words will be true and the epic will endure as long as mountains and rivers exist.

beats.title: The Composition and Summary of the Ramayana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Ramayana (Valmiki) describes the composition of the Ramayana by Valmiki after hearing the story from Narada. It then lists the entire narrative arc of the Ramayana in a condensed form, from Rama's birth to the burning of Lanka.

beats.title: Composition and Recitation of the Ramayana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Ramayana describes the composition of the epic by Valmiki, its recitation by Kuśa and Lava, and the praise of the sages. It covers the events from the burning of Lanka to Rama's coronation, including the building of the bridge, the killing of Ravana, and the reunion with Sita.

beats.title: The Gathering at Kurukshetra and the City of Ayodhya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the gathering of armies at Kurukshetra, with Dhritarashtra asking Sanjaya about the events. Sanjaya narrates Duryodhana's address to Dronacharya, listing prominent warriors on both sides, the blowing of conches, and the beginning of the battle. It also includes a segment from the Ramayana describing the city of Ayodhya and the lineage of Ikshvaku kings.

beats.title: Description of Ayodhya and King Dasharatha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the city of Ayodhya in detail, highlighting its prosperity, beauty, and the virtuous qualities of its inhabitants under the rule of King Dasharatha. It also introduces the king's lineage and his adherence to dharma.

beats.title: Description of Ayodhya and King Dasharatha's Court
beats.summary: This chapter describes the city of Ayodhya under King Dasharatha's rule, highlighting its prosperity, virtuous citizens, and capable ministers. It also lists the eight ministers and two chief priests who assist the king.

beats.title: Dasharatha's Quest for an Heir
beats.summary: King Dasharatha, ruling with virtuous ministers, desires a son. Sumantra recounts the prophecy of Sanatkumara about Rishyashringa, who will bring rain and beget sons for the king.

beats.title: The Luring of ṛśyaśṛṅga
beats.summary: King Daśaratha asks Sumantra to recount how ṛśyaśṛṅga was brought to the kingdom. Sumantra narrates that King Lomapāda, with his ministers and priest, devised a plan to lure the hermit ṛśyaśṛṅga, who had never seen women, using beautiful courtesans. The courtesans approached ṛśyaśṛṅga in the forest, offered him fruits and sweets, and embraced him, causing him to become infatuated and distressed after they left.

beats.title: The Prophecy and the Quest for Rishyashringa
beats.summary: King Dasharatha, advised by sage Sanatkumara's prophecy, seeks the sage Rishyashringa to perform a sacrifice for obtaining sons. The narrative recounts Rishyashringa's arrival at the court of King Lomapada of Anga, his marriage to Shanta, and the eventual invitation by Dasharatha to perform the putrakameshti yajna.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Return and Preparation for the Aśvamedha
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, after a long stay with King Lomapāda, returns to Ayodhyā with the sage Ṛṣiputra (R̥ṣyaśr̥ṅga) and his daughter Śāntā. The city celebrates their arrival. Later, Daśaratha, desiring sons, requests Ṛṣiputra to perform a horse sacrifice. The sage agrees and instructs preparations. Daśaratha summons priests including Suyajña, Vāmadeva, Jābāli, Kāśyapa, and Vasiṣṭha, and announces his intent to perform the Aśvamedha.

beats.title: Preparation for the Putrakameshti Yajna
beats.summary: King Dasharatha, advised by sage Rishyasringa and the assembly of brahmins led by Vashistha, prepares for a putrakameshti yajna (sacrifice for sons). He orders the collection of materials, release of the horse, and construction of altars and dwellings. The king emphasizes meticulous adherence to Vedic rites to avoid interference from brahmarakshasas.

beats.title: Preparation for Daśaratha's Sacrifice
beats.summary: King Daśaratha prepares for a great sacrifice (putrakāmeṣṭi) to obtain sons. Sage Vasiṣṭha instructs the minister Sumantra to invite kings and dignitaries from all over India, including Janaka of Mithilā, the king of Kāśī, the king of Kekaya, and Lomapāda of Aṅga. After the guests arrive, the sacrifice begins on the northern bank of the Sarayū river, with Ṛśyaśṛṅga as the chief priest.

beats.title: Preparation for the Horse Sacrifice
beats.summary: This chapter describes the preparations for King Dasharatha's horse sacrifice (ashvamedha) on the northern bank of the Sarayu River. Led by Rishyashringa, the sages perform the rituals flawlessly. The chapter details the generous distribution of food and gifts, the construction of sacrificial posts, and the arrangement of the fire altar in the shape of Garuda. Various animals are tied to the posts as per scriptural injunctions.

beats.title: Dasharatha's Ashvamedha and the Prophecy of Sons
beats.summary: King Dasharatha performs the Ashvamedha sacrifice under the guidance of Rishyashringa and Vasishtha. The ritual includes the horse sacrifice, distribution of dakshina to the priests, and a prophecy that Dasharatha will have four sons.

beats.title: The Gods Seek Vishnu's Help to Destroy Ravana
beats.summary: The gods and sages, oppressed by Ravana, approach Brahma for a solution. Brahma reveals that Ravana is invulnerable to gods, demons, and others but not to humans due to his neglect. Vishnu is then requested to incarnate as the son of Dasharatha to kill Ravana.

beats.title: The Birth of Rama
beats.summary: Ravana obtains a boon from Brahma that he cannot be killed by any being except humans. The gods approach Vishnu, who decides to incarnate as the son of Dasharatha. A divine being appears at Dasharatha's sacrifice and gives him a bowl of divine payasa to distribute among his queens, resulting in the birth of Rama and his brothers.

beats.title: The Birth of the Vanaras
beats.summary: Brahma commands the gods, sages, and celestial beings to create powerful, shape-shifting monkey warriors to assist Vishnu in his incarnation as Rama, who will kill Ravana. The monkeys are born from various female beings, including apsaras, gandharvis, yakshinis, naginis, rkshis, vidyadharis, kinnaris, and vanaris. Among them, Sugriva (son of Surya) and Valin (son of Indra) become leaders. After the horse sacrifice, King Dasharatha returns to his city.

beats.title: Birth of the Princes and Arrival of Vishwamitra
beats.summary: King Dasharatha returns to Ayodhya with Rishyasringa and Shanta. Kausalya gives birth to Rama, an incarnation of half of Vishnu. Kaikeyi gives birth to Bharata, a quarter of Vishnu. Sumitra gives birth to Lakshmana and Shatrughna, each half of Vishnu. The sage Vasishtha performs naming ceremonies. The four princes grow up virtuous. Lakshmana is especially devoted to Rama. King Dasharatha begins to consider their marriages. The sage Vishwamitra arrives seeking an audience.

beats.title: Vishvamitra's Request
beats.summary: King Dasharatha welcomes sage Vishvamitra with great honor. Vishvamitra requests Dasharatha to send his eldest son Rama to protect his sacrificial ritual from the demons Maricha and Subahu, who have been disrupting it. Dasharatha agrees.

beats.title: Vishvamitra's Request for Rama
beats.summary: King Dasharatha, in response to sage Vishvamitra's request to send Rama to protect his yajna from demons, expresses reluctance due to Rama's youth and inexperience. Vishvamitra insists on Rama's capability and divine destiny, eventually persuading Dasharatha to agree.

beats.title: Dasharatha's Reluctance and Vasistha's Counsel
beats.summary: King Dasharatha expresses fear of Ravana and refuses to send Rama with Vishvamitra. Vishvamitra becomes angry, and sage Vasistha intervenes, convincing Dasharatha to allow Rama to go, praising Vishvamitra's power and knowledge of weapons.

beats.title: Vishvamitra's Instruction and the Gift of Bala and Atibala
beats.summary: Vishvamitra explains to King Dasharatha the origin of divine weapons, describing how they were created by Kṛśāśva's sons and given to Vishvamitra. He then takes Rama and Lakshmana to the Sarayu riverbank, where he teaches them the Bala and Atibala mantras, granting them immunity from fatigue, fever, and demons.

beats.title: The Story of Kāma's Destruction and the Arrival at Kāmāśrama
beats.summary: Viśvāmitra tells Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa the story of Kāma (Cupid) being burned to ashes by Śiva's third eye at this āśrama, now called Kāmāśrama. The sages there welcome them.

beats.title: Crossing the Sarayu and Entering the Forest
beats.summary: Rama and Lakshmana, accompanied by Vishvamitra, cross the Sarayu river and enter a dense forest. Vishvamitra explains the origin of the Malada and Karusha regions, which were created from Indra's bodily impurities after the slaying of Vritra. He then describes the yakshini Tataka, who terrorizes the area.

beats.title: The Origin of Tāṭakā and the Command to Slay Her
beats.summary: Viśvāmitra narrates the origin of Tāṭakā, a yakṣī who was given the strength of a thousand elephants by Brahmā, married to Sunda, and later cursed by Agastya along with her son Mārīca, becoming a rākṣasī. He urges Rāma to kill her for the welfare of cows and brahmins, citing precedents of Indra and Viṣṇu slaying women for dharma.

beats.title: The Slaying of Tataka
beats.summary: Rama, accompanied by Lakshmana and sage Vishvamitra, encounters the demoness Tataka in the forest. After a brief hesitation due to her being a woman, Rama kills her with an arrow, pleasing the gods and Vishvamitra. The sage then promises to bestow divine weapons upon Rama.

beats.title: The Bestowal of Divine Weapons
beats.summary: In this chapter, the sage Vishvamitra grants Rama a vast array of divine weapons, including celestial astras and various weapons associated with gods and demons. Rama receives them with reverence, and the weapons themselves appear before him, declaring themselves his servants. The chapter concludes with Rama preparing to depart.

beats.title: Rama Receives Weapons and Learns of Siddhashrama
beats.summary: Rama, having received divine weapons from Vishvamitra, asks for their withdrawal methods. Vishvamitra teaches him the samhara (withdrawal) mantras, and the personified weapons appear before Rama, offering service. Rama dismisses them, instructing them to assist him when needed. As they continue, Rama sees a beautiful forest and asks about it. Vishvamitra explains it is the Siddhashrama, the former hermitage of Vamana, where the gods once sought Vishnu's help against Bali.

beats.title: Vamana's Incarnation and the Protection of Siddhashrama
beats.summary: In this chapter, Vishnu incarnates as Vamana, approaches Bali (Vairocani), begs for three paces of land, then expands to cover the three worlds and restores them to Indra. The sages of Siddhashrama honor Vishvamitra and the princes Rama and Lakshmana. Vishvamitra enters a six-day initiation (diksha) during which Rama and Lakshmana guard the hermitage. On the sixth day, two rakshasas attack, creating a fearful noise.

beats.title: Rama Protects Vishvamitra's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Rama and Lakshmana, guided by Vishvamitra, defeat the demons Maricha and Subahu who were disrupting the sage's sacrifice. After the successful protection of the yajna, the sages invite Rama to witness King Janaka's great sacrifice and the wondrous bow.

beats.title: Departure from Siddhāśrama and the Tale of Kuśa's Sons
beats.summary: Viśvāmitra and the sages depart from Siddhāśrama, traveling north. They camp on the Śoṇā riverbank. Rāma asks about the history of the region, and Viśvāmitra narrates the story of King Kuśa and his four sons who founded cities, including the lineage of Kuśanābha and his hundred daughters.

beats.title: The Hundred Daughters of Kuśanābha and the Wind God's Wrath
beats.summary: King Kuśanābha's hundred daughters are approached by the wind god Vāyu, who demands they become his wives. They refuse, citing their father's authority. Enraged, Vāyu cripples them. The daughters report to their father, who praises their forbearance. Meanwhile, the sage Cūlī performs penance, attended by the gandharvī Somadā.

beats.title: The Lineage of Kuśa and the Birth of Gādhi
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Kuśanābha, his hundred daughters who were cursed by Vāyu to become hunchbacks, their marriage to Brahmadatta, and the birth of Gādhi. It also introduces the lineage of Viśvāmitra (Kauśika) and the origin of the river Kauśikī.

beats.title: The Origin of the Ganga
beats.summary: In this chapter, Sage Vishvamitra narrates the origin of the Ganga river to Rama. He describes his own lineage, the arrival at the Ganga, and the story of Himavan's daughters Ganga and Uma.

beats.title: The Origin of Ganga and the Marriage of Uma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Ramayana (Valmiki) describes the origin of the river Ganga and her sister Uma. Himavan, the king of mountains, has two daughters: Ganga (the elder) and Uma. The gods request Ganga for their purposes, and Himavan gives her away. Later, Uma is given in marriage to Rudra (Shiva). The chapter also recounts the story of Shiva and Uma's prolonged union, which threatens the worlds, leading the gods to request Shiva to withhold his seed. Shiva releases his seed onto the earth, which Agni receives.

beats.title: The Birth of Skanda
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the origin of Skanda (Kartikeya) as the commander of the gods. It begins with the gods seeking a leader to defeat the demons. Agni, on the gods' request, impregnates Ganga, who bears Skanda. Parvati (Shailaputri) curses the gods and the earth due to being prevented from having a child. The gods then approach Brahma, who advises them to have Agni beget a son on Ganga. Skanda is born in a reedy lake (Sharavana) and becomes the general of the gods.

beats.title: Birth of Skanda and the Quest of Sagara
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the birth of Skanda (Kartikeya) from Agni and Ganga, his nurturing by the Krittikas, and his rise as the commander of the divine army. It also begins the story of King Sagara's quest for progeny.

beats.title: The Birth of Sagara's Sons and the Horse Sacrifice
beats.summary: Sagara's queens Keśinī and Sumatī receive a boon from Bhṛgu: Keśinī bears one son (Asamañja), Sumatī bears 60,000 sons from a gourd. Asamañja is cruel and exiled; his son Aṃśumān is virtuous. Sagara decides to perform a horse sacrifice, with Aṃśumān as guardian. The sacrifice is set between Himavān and Vindhya mountains.

beats.title: The Digging of the Earth and the Destruction of Sagara's Sons
beats.summary: King Sagara's horse sacrifice is disrupted when Indra, in a rakshasa form, steals the sacrificial horse. Sagara orders his 60,000 sons to search for the horse by digging the earth. They dig through the earth, causing destruction, and eventually reach the underworld where they encounter Kapila (an incarnation of Vasudeva). The gods, alarmed, appeal to Brahma, who foretells the destruction of Sagara's sons due to their digging. The sons are later destroyed by Kapila's wrath.

beats.title: The Digging of the Earth and the Destruction of Sagara's Sons
beats.summary: King Sagara's 60,000 sons dig through the earth in search of the sacrificial horse, encountering four directional guardian elephants. They eventually find the horse near sage Kapila, who burns them to ashes with his gaze. Sagara then instructs his grandson Amshuman to continue the search.

beats.title: Amshuman's Quest for the Horse and the Fate of Sagara's Sons
beats.summary: King Sagara, having learned that his sons have not returned, instructs his grandson Amshuman to find the horse and the sons. Amshuman ventures into the underworld, encounters directional elephants, and learns from Garuda that the sons were burned by Kapila's wrath. Garuda advises that only Ganga can purify their ashes. Amshuman retrieves the horse and returns to Sagara, who completes the sacrifice but remains uncertain about bringing Ganga to earth.

beats.title: The Descent of the Ganga: The Penance of Bhagiratha
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the lineage of the Ikshvaku kings after Sagara, focusing on the efforts of Bhagiratha to bring the Ganga to earth to purify the ashes of his ancestors, the sons of Sagara. It describes the reigns of Asamanja, Amshuman, Dilipa, and Bhagiratha, and Bhagiratha's penance to obtain the boon from Brahma.

beats.title: The Descent of the Ganga
beats.summary: King Bhagiratha convinces Shiva to receive the descending Ganga on his head. Shiva holds Ganga in his matted locks for many years, then releases her to flow to the earth. The river's descent is witnessed by gods and sages, and its waters purify all who touch them. Bhagiratha leads Ganga to the ocean.

beats.title: The Descent of Ganga
beats.summary: King Bhagiratha brings the river Ganga to earth to purify the ashes of his ancestors, the 60,000 sons of Sagara. Brahma appears and declares the ancestors liberated, and Ganga becomes known as Bhagirathi. The story is narrated by Vishvamitra to Rama and Lakshmana.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean
beats.summary: Viśvāmitra narrates the story of the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana) to Rāma, explaining the origin of the Apsarās, Vāruṇī, Uccaiḥśravas, Kaustubha, and Amṛta, and how the distinction between Suras and Asuras arose.

beats.title: The Birth of the Maruts
beats.summary: This chapter from the Ramayana (Valmiki) narrates the story of Diti, who after her sons (Daityas) were killed by the Adityas (led by Indra), seeks a powerful son to kill Indra. She performs severe penance under the guidance of her husband Kashyapa, but Indra serves her and eventually exploits her impurity to enter her womb and split the fetus into seven parts, creating the Maruts.

beats.title: The Birth of the Maruts and the Lineage of Viśālā
beats.summary: This chapter from the Ramayana recounts the story of Diti's pregnancy with the Maruts, Indra's intervention, and the subsequent blessing that the seven fragments become the Maruts. It also describes the lineage of the kings of Viśālā, culminating in King Sumati, who welcomes Viśvāmitra and Rāma.

beats.title: Rama and Lakshmana Meet Sumati; Story of Ahalya's Seduction
beats.summary: King Sumati asks Sage Vishvamitra about two divine-looking youths who have arrived at his hermitage. Vishvamitra explains they are Rama and Lakshmana, sons of King Dasharatha, who have been staying at Siddhashrama and killing rakshasas. Sumati honors them, and they proceed to Mithila. On the way, Rama sees an abandoned hermitage and asks about it. Vishvamitra narrates the story of Gautama's hermitage, where Indra seduced Ahalya, Gautama's wife, and was cursed.

beats.title: The Curse of Gautama and Redemption of Ahalya
beats.summary: Gautama curses Indra for impersonating him and seducing Ahalya, making Indra lose his testicles and Ahalya become an invisible ascetic. Indra later receives ram's testicles from the gods. Ahalya is redeemed by Rama's visit.

beats.title: Arrival at Mithila
beats.summary: Rama and Lakshmana, guided by Vishvamitra, arrive at King Janaka's sacrificial grounds. Janaka welcomes them and expresses gratitude for Vishvamitra's presence. He inquires about the two divine-looking youths, Rama and Lakshmana.

beats.title: Janaka's Inquiry and Satānanda's Welcome
beats.summary: King Janaka asks Sage Visvamitra about the two divine-looking youths (Rama and Lakshmana) who have arrived at his court. Visvamitra narrates their identity as sons of King Dasaratha, their stay at Siddhasrama, the killing of rakshasas, their visit to Visala, and the meeting with Ahalya and Gautama. Satānanda, Gautama's son, then expresses joy at seeing Rama and inquires about his mother Ahalya's redemption. Visvamitra confirms the events, and Satānanda welcomes Rama, praising Visvamitra's greatness.

beats.title: Viśvāmitra's Visit to Vasiṣṭha's Hermitage
beats.summary: King Viśvāmitra, while touring his kingdom, visits the hermitage of the sage Vasiṣṭha. He is warmly welcomed and they exchange courteous inquiries about each other's well-being, discussing dharma and kingship.

beats.title: The Request for Śabalā
beats.summary: King Viśvāmitra, after being hosted by sage Vasiṣṭha, is impressed by the divine cow Śabalā and requests her in exchange for vast wealth. Vasiṣṭha refuses, stating that Śabalā is inseparable from him like his own reputation.

beats.title: The Gathering on Kurukshetra
beats.summary: King Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya what his sons and the Pandavas did on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. Sanjaya describes the armies, listing warriors on both sides, and narrates the blowing of conches signaling the start of war.

beats.title: Vasishta's Divine Cow Destroys Visvamitra's Army
beats.summary: Vasishta's divine cow creates armies to destroy Visvamitra's forces, leading to Visvamitra's defeat and his retreat to the Himalayas for penance to please Shiva.

beats.title: Viśvāmitra's Arrogance and Vasiṣṭha's Power
beats.summary: Viśvāmitra, having received divine weapons from Śiva, becomes arrogant and attacks Vasiṣṭha's hermitage. Vasiṣṭha, with his spiritual power, neutralizes all of Viśvāmitra's weapons and curses him, declaring he will cease to be a kṣatriya.

beats.title: Viśvāmitra's Penance for Brahminhood
beats.summary: Viśvāmitra, after being humiliated by Vasiṣṭha's Brahmadaṇḍa, resolves to become a Brahmarshi through severe penance. He goes south with his wife, performs tapas for a thousand years, and begets sons. Brahmā appears and declares him a Rājarṣi, but Viśvāmitra, unsatisfied, continues his penance.

beats.title: Triśaṅku's Quest for Heaven
beats.summary: King Triśaṅku, desiring to perform a sacrifice to ascend to heaven in his physical body, is rejected by his guru Vasiṣṭha. He then approaches Vasiṣṭha's sons, who also refuse and curse him to become a caṇḍāla. Triśaṅku, transformed into a caṇḍāla, seeks refuge with Viśvāmitra.

beats.title: Vishvamitra's Compassion for the Cursed King
beats.summary: King Kalmashapada, cursed to become a cannibal, approaches sage Vishvamitra for help. Vishvamitra compassionately accepts him and arranges for a grand sacrifice to restore his form, sending his disciples to invite all sages except those from Vasishta's lineage.

beats.title: Viśvāmitra's Curse and Triśaṅku's Ascent
beats.summary: Viśvāmitra, enraged by the insults of the Vāsiṣṭhas, curses them to become outcastes. He then proceeds to perform a sacrifice for Triśaṅku to ascend to heaven in his physical body. When the gods do not appear, Viśvāmitra uses his own power to send Triśaṅku to heaven.

beats.title: Triśaṅku's Ascent and Viśvāmitra's New Creation
beats.summary: King Triśaṅku, cursed by his guru, attempts to ascend to heaven in his physical body. Sage Viśvāmitra, through his tapas, helps him rise, but Indra casts him down. Viśvāmitra, enraged, creates a new constellation and threatens to create a new Indra. The gods and sages appease him, allowing Triśaṅku to remain in the sky as a constellation, head-downwards. Viśvāmitra then moves to Puṣkaras to continue his austerities.

beats.title: The Story of Śunaḥśepa
beats.summary: King Ambarīṣa of Ayodhyā, while performing a sacrifice, loses the sacrificial animal due to Indra's theft. The priest declares the king negligent and demands a human substitute. Ambarīṣa searches far and wide and eventually approaches the sage Ṛcīka at Bhṛgutuṅga, offering to buy one of his sons. Ṛcīka refuses to sell his eldest, the mother refuses to part with her youngest, but the middle son Śunaḥśepa volunteers. Ambarīṣa buys him for a hundred thousand cows and returns. On the way, Śunaḥśepa, exhausted and distressed, meets the sage Viśvāmitra at Puṣkara and begs for protection.

beats.title: The Rescue of Śunaḥśepa and Viśvāmitra's Penance
beats.summary: Viśvāmitra rescues Śunaḥśepa from being sacrificed in King Ambarīṣa's yajña, curses his own sons for refusing to help, and continues his penance to become a ṛṣi.

beats.title: Viśvāmitra's Penance and the Gods' Interference
beats.summary: Viśvāmitra, after being interrupted by Menakā, resumes severe penance on the northern mountain. The gods, fearing his power, send Rambhā to seduce him. Viśvāmitra resists and continues his tapas, eventually earning the title Maharṣi from Brahmā, but he seeks the higher title of Brahmarshi, which requires complete mastery of the senses.

beats.title: The Seduction of Vishvamitra and the Curse of Rambha
beats.summary: Indra sends the apsara Rambha to seduce the sage Vishvamitra and break his tapas. Vishvamitra, enraged, curses Rambha to become a stone for 10,000 years, but promises she will be liberated by a great Brahmana. He then leaves the Himalayas and performs severe austerities in the east, causing cosmic disturbances. The gods, alarmed, seek Brahma's intervention.

beats.title: Vishvamitra's Brahmarṣi Attainment and Janaka's Gratitude
beats.summary: King Janaka, after hearing the story of Vishvamitra's attainment of brahmarṣi status from Śatānanda, expresses his gratitude and invites Vishvamitra to return the next day. Vishvamitra then departs with Rama and Lakshmana.

beats.title: The History of Shiva's Bow and Sita's Birth
beats.summary: King Janaka recounts the history of the divine bow of Shiva, which was given as a trust to his ancestor Devarata. He explains how his daughter Sita was born from the earth while he was plowing, and how he set the condition that she would be wed only to one who could string the bow. Many kings failed and besieged Mithila, but the gods granted Janaka a fourfold army to defeat them. Now, at the request of the sage Vishvamitra, Janaka agrees to show the bow to Rama and Lakshmana.

beats.title: The Breaking of the Bow
beats.summary: King Janaka orders his ministers to bring the divine bow. Viśvāmitra instructs Rāma to look at the bow. Rāma effortlessly lifts, strings, and breaks the bow, fulfilling the condition for Sītā's hand. Janaka declares Sītā will be given to Rāma.

beats.title: Janaka's Invitation
beats.summary: King Janaka sends messengers to King Dasharatha, informing him that his daughter Sita, won by Rama in the bow-breaking contest, is to be married to Rama. He invites Dasharatha to Mithila for the wedding. Dasharatha, overjoyed, consults his ministers and sages, and decides to depart the next day.

beats.title: Arrival of Dasharatha and Preparations for the Wedding
beats.summary: King Janaka welcomes King Dasharatha and his entourage for the wedding of Rama and Sita. Janaka expresses joy at the alliance and arranges for the ceremony. He sends for his brother Kushadhvaja to join the festivities.

beats.title: The Ikshvaku Dynasty Genealogy
beats.summary: King Janaka sends his minister Sudamana to invite King Dasharatha to Mithila. Dasharatha arrives with his retinue and sages. Vasistha recounts the Ikshvaku dynasty lineage from Brahma to Rama and Lakshmana.

beats.title: The Lineage of Janaka and the Marriage of Sītā
beats.summary: King Janaka narrates the lineage of the kings of Mithila to the sage Viśvāmitra, culminating in his own birth and that of his brother Kuśadhvaja. He recounts his defeat of King Sudhanvan and his decision to give his daughters Sītā and Ūrmilā in marriage to Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa.

beats.title: The Marriage Arrangements of Rama and His Brothers
beats.summary: King Janaka agrees to the marriage of his daughters Sita and Urmila to Rama and Lakshmana, and also arranges the marriage of his brother Kuśadhvaja's daughters to Bharata and Śatrughna. The wedding is set for the third day on the Uttara Phalgunī constellation. King Daśaratha performs a cow-gift ceremony.

beats.title: The Marriage of Daśaratha's Sons
beats.summary: King Janaka performs a grand cow-giving ceremony for his sons. Yudhājit, Bharata's maternal uncle, arrives from Kekaya to see his nephew. The next day, Daśaratha and his sons, accompanied by sages, proceed to the sacrificial ground. Vasiṣṭha informs Janaka that Daśaratha awaits him as the giver. Janaka replies that he is ready and asks why the delay. He then offers his daughters: Sītā to Rāma, Ūrmilā to Lakṣmaṇa, Māṇḍavī to Bharata, and Śrutakīrtī to Śatrughna.

beats.title: The Wedding of Rama and His Brothers
beats.summary: The wedding of Rama and his brothers to the daughters of Janaka is completed with Vasishta's guidance. After the ceremony, Visvamitra departs to the northern mountain. Janaka gives immense dowry. On the return journey to Ayodhya, a terrible storm and darkness arise, revealing a fearsome ascetic resembling Kailasa and the fire of doom.

beats.title: Parashurama's Challenge
beats.summary: Parashurama confronts Rama after the breaking of Shiva's bow, challenging him to string Vishnu's bow. He recounts the history of the two divine bows and the past conflict between Shiva and Vishnu. Dasharatha pleads for mercy, but Parashurama insists on a duel.

beats.title: Parashurama's Challenge and Rama's Restraint
beats.summary: Parashurama recounts the history of the divine bow and arrow, originally belonging to Vishnu, which came into his possession through a lineage of sages. He challenges Rama to string the bow and shoot an arrow, but Rama, after stringing it, refrains from killing Parashurama out of respect for his brahmin status and Viśvāmitra's request. Parashurama acknowledges Rama's superiority and departs.

beats.title: Rama's Victory over Parasurama and Return to Ayodhya
beats.summary: Rama defeats Parasurama (Jamadagnya) in a contest of archery, then returns to Ayodhya with his father and army, marries Sita and other princesses, and lives happily.

beats.title: The Gathering at Kurukshetra and Rama's Virtues
beats.summary: This chapter describes the gathering of armies at Kurukshetra, Duryodhana's address to Dronacharya listing the warriors on both sides, the blowing of conches signaling the start of war, and a flashback to Rama's virtues and his relationship with Sita, as well as Bharata's departure to his maternal uncle's kingdom.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Decision to Crown Rāma
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, feeling aged and desiring to install his virtuous son Rāma as heir, convenes an assembly of nobles and citizens. He praises Rāma's exceptional qualities—wisdom, valor, humility, and compassion—and expresses his wish to see Rāma crowned before his own death.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Proposal to Install Rāma as Yuvarāja
beats.summary: King Daśaratha expresses his desire to retire and install Rāma as yuvarāja (crown prince). The assembly of nobles and citizens praises Rāma's virtues and supports the proposal.

beats.title: Preparation for Rāma's Consecration
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, urged by the citizens and sages, decides to consecrate Rāma as heir apparent (yauvarājya). He instructs his ministers to prepare for the ceremony in the auspicious month of Caitra. Sumantra brings Rāma to the court, where Rāma bows to his father. Daśaratha, overjoyed, offers Rāma a splendid seat, and the assembly shines with Rāma's presence.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Decision to Consecrate Rāma
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, pleased with Rāma, announces his decision to consecrate Rāma as heir apparent (yauvarājya). He advises Rāma on righteous conduct and governance. Rāma returns home, and the citizens rejoice. Daśaratha then instructs his charioteer to bring Rāma back for further discussion, and Rāma returns to the palace.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Decision to Consecrate Rāma
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, feeling old and seeing inauspicious omens, decides to consecrate Rāma as yuvarāja (heir apparent) the next day. He instructs Rāma to fast with Sītā and be guarded. Rāma informs his mother Kausalyā, who is meditating on Janārdana.

beats.title: Preparations for Rāma's Coronation
beats.summary: King Daśaratha instructs Vasiṣṭha to prepare Rāma and Sītā for the coronation. Rāma receives blessings from Kausalyā and instructs Lakṣmaṇa to rule the kingdom. Vasiṣṭha performs the ritual fast with Rāma and Sītā, then returns to the palace.

beats.title: Preparations for Rama's Coronation
beats.summary: The chapter describes the preparations for Rama's coronation in Ayodhya. The city is bustling with excited citizens, the royal priest Vasishta visits the king, and Rama performs a ritual worship of Narayana with Sita before spending the night in Vishnu's temple. The next morning, the city is decorated for the ceremony.

beats.title: The People's Joy and Manthara's Wrath
beats.summary: The people of Ayodhya joyfully prepare for Rama's coronation, decorating the city and gathering from all directions. Manthara, a hunchbacked maid, sees the preparations and, enraged, goes to Queen Kaikeyi to poison her mind against Rama's consecration.

beats.title: Manthara's Manipulation
beats.summary: Manthara, a hunchbacked maid, incites Queen Kaikeyi against Rama's coronation by poisoning her mind with suspicion and fear, leading Kaikeyi to demand Rama's exile.

beats.title: Manthara's Manipulation
beats.summary: Manthara, the hunchbacked maid, convinces Queen Kaikeyi that Rama's coronation will lead to the downfall of her son Bharata. She argues that Rama, being the eldest and virtuous, will become king and may harm Bharata. Manthara urges Kaikeyi to demand Rama's exile and Bharata's coronation to protect her son's future.

beats.title: Manthara's Counsel and Kaikeyi's Resolve
beats.summary: Kaikeyi, incited by Manthara, decides to demand two boons from King Dasharatha: the exile of Rama to the forest for fourteen years and the coronation of her son Bharata. Manthara reminds Kaikeyi of the boons promised during the Devasura war and advises her to feign anger and enter the krodhagriha.

beats.title: Manthara's Incitement and Kaikeyi's Demand
beats.summary: Kaikeyi, incited by Manthara, decides to demand Rama's exile and Bharata's coronation. Manthara flatters Kaikeyi, who then removes her ornaments and lies on the floor in a rage, threatening to die unless her demands are met.

beats.title: Kaikeyi's Boon and Rama's Exile
beats.summary: King Dasharatha, after promising Kaikeyi a boon, finds her lying on the ground in distress. He tries to console her and offers to fulfill any wish, even at the cost of his life. Kaikeyi then extracts a promise and reveals her demand: Rama's exile and Bharata's coronation.

beats.title: Kaikeyī's Boons and Rāma's Exile
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, bound by his promise to Kaikeyī, is forced to exile Rāma to the forest for fourteen years and install Bharata as heir. He laments and collapses, but Kaikeyī insists on her boons.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Lament and Kaikeyī's Cruelty
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, grief-stricken after Rāma's exile, laments to Kaikeyī, pleading with her to relent. He cites examples of truthfulness from history, but Kaikeyī remains adamant, demanding Rāma's exile and Bharata's coronation. Daśaratha collapses in despair.

beats.title: Sumantra Sent to Fetch Rāma
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, bound by his promise to Kaikeyī, orders the charioteer Sumantra to bring Rāma. Sumantra, misunderstanding the king's sorrow, joyfully goes to fetch Rāma, thinking it is for his coronation. Meanwhile, preparations for Rāma's coronation are underway, with brahmins, ministers, and citizens gathering, and all auspicious items assembled.

beats.title: Sumantra Summoned to Rama
beats.summary: Sumantra, the charioteer, is sent by King Dasharatha to summon Rama. He goes to Rama's palace, observes its splendor, and finds Rama seated with Sita. Sumantra bows and delivers the king's message.

beats.title: Rama's Journey to the Palace
beats.summary: Rama, informed by Sumantra that his father King Dasaratha and Queen Kaikeyi wish to see him, joyfully prepares to go. He tells Sita that the king and queen are likely discussing his coronation. Rama mounts a splendid chariot and proceeds through Ayodhya, accompanied by Lakshmana. The citizens praise him, and he observes the bustling city.

beats.title: Rama's Inquiry into Dasaratha's Grief
beats.summary: Rama enters the palace and finds his father King Dasaratha seated with Kaikeyi, looking distressed. Rama respectfully greets them and inquires about the cause of his father's sorrow, expressing his devotion and concern.

beats.title: Kaikeyi's Demand and Rama's Acceptance of Exile
beats.summary: Kaikeyi reveals the two boons she had secured from King Dasharatha long ago, demanding that Bharata be crowned and Rama be exiled to the forest for fourteen years. Rama calmly accepts, promising to fulfill his father's vow, and asks Kaikeyi to send for Bharata.

beats.title: Rama's Exile Begins
beats.summary: Rama, urged by Kaikeyi, prepares to go into exile. He reassures Kaikeyi of his commitment to dharma, bids farewell to his father and mother, and leaves the palace with Lakshmana.

beats.title: Rama's Announcement of Exile to Kausalya
beats.summary: Rama enters Kausalya's inner chambers, finds her performing a fire sacrifice for his well-being, and informs her of his impending exile to the forest for fourteen years, causing her great distress.

beats.title: Kausalya's Lament and Lakshmana's Vow
beats.summary: Kausalya laments Rama's exile, expressing her grief and anger at Kaikeyi's manipulation. Lakshmana consoles her, vowing to oppose the injustice and protect Rama.

beats.title: Kausalyā's Lament and Rāma's Resolve
beats.summary: Kausalyā, weeping and distressed, pleads with Rāma not to go to the forest, citing her love and the dharma of serving one's mother. Rāma explains he cannot disobey his father's command, citing examples of sages and ancestors who followed their father's orders. He reassures her he will return after fulfilling his promise.

beats.title: Rama's Resolve to Go into Exile
beats.summary: Rama explains to Lakshmana that he must renounce the kingdom and go to the forest to fulfill his father's promise to Kaikeyi, accepting it as fate. He instructs Lakshmana to stop the coronation preparations and prepares for exile.

beats.title: Lakshmana's Outburst Against Fate
beats.summary: Lakshmana, angered by Rama's acceptance of exile, argues against fate and advocates for action. He criticizes Rama's passive reliance on destiny, asserting that a true warrior overcomes fate through personal effort. Lakshmana vows to defeat any opposition to Rama's coronation, including their father, and proposes to rule in Rama's stead during his exile.

beats.title: Lakshmana's Anger and Rama's Counsel
beats.summary: In this chapter, Lakshmana expresses his anger and determination to prevent Rama's exile, offering to fight the king's forces. Rama calms him, emphasizing obedience to their father. Kausalya laments Rama's exile and wishes to accompany him, but Rama advises her to serve the king.

beats.title: Kausalyā's Grief and Blessing
beats.summary: In this chapter, Kausalyā, overwhelmed by grief at Rāma's impending exile, initially begs him to take her with him. Rāma consoles her, emphasizing the supreme authority of the king and the duty of a wife to serve her husband. He instructs her to honor the king and await his return. Kausalyā ultimately blesses Rāma, invoking various deities and natural forces for his protection during his forest exile.

beats.title: Kausalya's Blessings and Rama's Departure
beats.summary: In this chapter, Kausalya performs a svastyayana (auspicious ritual) for Rama before his departure to the forest, invoking blessings from various deities and beings. Rama then leaves, and Sita notices his dejected demeanor and questions him.

beats.title: Rama's Instructions to Sita Before Exile
beats.summary: Rama explains to Sita the reason for his exile: his father Dasharatha had granted two boons to Kaikeyi, who now demands Rama's exile and Bharata's coronation. Rama instructs Sita to stay in Ayodhya, honor his parents, and serve Bharata and his other mothers. Sita becomes angry and rebukes Rama.

beats.title: Sita's Plea to Accompany Rama into Exile
beats.summary: Sita pleads with Rama to allow her to accompany him into exile, arguing that a wife's duty is to share her husband's fate. Rama describes the hardships of forest life to dissuade her.

beats.title: Sita's Determination to Accompany Rama to the Forest
beats.summary: Sita responds to Rama's description of forest hardships, asserting her determination to accompany him. She argues that the forest's difficulties are actually virtues from her perspective, and that her duty as a wife is to follow her husband. She recalls hearing from brahmins that she was destined for forest life, and insists on fulfilling that prophecy.

beats.title: Sita's Plea to Accompany Rama into Exile
beats.summary: Sita pleads with Rama to allow her to accompany him into exile, arguing that a wife's place is with her husband in all circumstances. She cites examples of devoted wives, expresses her determination to share his hardships, and threatens suicide if left behind.

beats.title: Sita's Determination and Preparations for Exile
beats.summary: In this chapter, Sita expresses her determination to accompany Rama to the forest, threatening to take poison if left behind. Rama consents, explaining his duty to obey his father's command. He instructs Sita to distribute wealth to brahmins and ascetics. Lakshmana volunteers to accompany them, assuring that Bharata will care for their mothers.

beats.title: Rama's Preparations for Exile: Gifts to Suyajna
beats.summary: Rama instructs Lakshmana to bring the brahmana Suyajna, and then honors him with gifts including jewelry, a bed, and an elephant, as Sita prompts. Suyajna blesses them. Rama then asks Lakshmana to honor Agastya and Kaushika.

beats.title: Rama's Generosity and Departure
beats.summary: Rama and Lakshmana, accompanied by Sita, distribute wealth and gifts to brahmins and dependents before departing to see their father. The chapter includes a humorous episode with the brahmin Trijata, who receives a cow and later throws a stick in jest. The people of Ayodhya mourn Rama's exile.

beats.title: Rama's Departure and the City's Lament
beats.summary: Rama prepares to leave for exile, and the citizens of Ayodhya express their grief and anger at Kaikeyi's actions. Rama remains calm and composed, instructing Sumantra to inform the king of his arrival.

beats.title: Rama's Departure
beats.summary: King Dasharatha, overwhelmed by grief, summons Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita to the palace. Despite the king's pleas to stay, Rama insists on leaving for the forest to uphold his father's promise to Kaikeyi. Rama consoles his father and departs with Lakshmana and Sita.

beats.title: The Exile of Rāma
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, bound by his promise to Kaikeyī, orders Rāma's exile. Rāma accepts gracefully, refusing kingship and comforts, choosing to uphold truth. Daśaratha instructs preparations for Rāma's departure, including wealth and army. Kaikeyī, fearing Rāma's return, insists on exile. Daśaratha rebukes her, citing the precedent of Sagara's son Asamañja who was exiled for cruelty.

beats.title: Rāma's Exile Begins
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, after hearing the citizens' complaint about his son Sagara's cruel act, decides to exile Rāma. Rāma accepts the exile calmly, dons ascetic garments, and Sītā struggles with the bark cloth. Rāma ties it for her, and the people lament. Daśaratha rebukes Kaikeyī and asks Rāma to care for Kausalyā.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Grief and Preparations for Rāma's Exile
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, overwhelmed with grief at Rāma's exile, orders Sumantra to prepare a chariot. He instructs that Rāma be taken beyond the kingdom. Sumantra brings the chariot, and Daśaratha gives ornaments and garments for Sītā. Sītā adorns herself, and her mother-in-law Kausalyā embraces her, advising her to remain devoted to Rāma regardless of his fortunes. Sītā respectfully agrees.

beats.title: Departure of Rama to the Forest
beats.summary: Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana take leave of King Dasaratha and the queens, including Kausalya and Sumitra. Sita expresses her devotion to Rama as her husband and god. Rama consoles Kausalya, and Lakshmana receives advice from his mother Sumitra. They then board the chariot driven by Sumantra to depart for the forest.

beats.title: Rama's Departure and the Grief of Ayodhya
beats.summary: Rama, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana, departs for the forest. The citizens of Ayodhya, including King Dasaratha and Queen Kausalya, grieve deeply. Rama urges the charioteer Sumantra to drive faster to avoid prolonging their sorrow.

beats.title: Rāma's Departure and the Grief of Ayodhyā
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, overwhelmed by grief, watches Rāma depart for exile. The city of Ayodhyā falls into despair, with natural omens and widespread lamentation. The king collapses when Rāma disappears from sight.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Lament
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, grief-stricken after Rāma's exile, collapses and laments. He rebukes Kaikeyī, then enters the city and his palace, crying out for Rāma. Kausalyā supports him as he falls.

beats.title: Kausalya's Lament
beats.summary: Kausalya laments the exile of her son Rama, blaming Kaikeyi for the treachery. She expresses deep sorrow and anxiety about Rama's life in the forest with Sita and Lakshmana. Sumitra consoles her, reminding her of Rama's virtues and the righteousness of his actions.

beats.title: Rama's Departure and Consolation
beats.summary: In this chapter, Kaushalya consoles Kaikeyi, praising Rama's virtues and predicting his return. Rama departs for the forest, followed by the citizens of Ayodhya. He urges them to serve Bharata and remain loyal to the kingdom.

beats.title: Rama's Departure and the Citizens' Plea
beats.summary: Rama, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana, departs for the forest. The citizens of Ayodhya, especially the Brahmins, plead with him to return, expressing their devotion and offering to follow him. Rama continues on foot, and they reach the Tamasa river at nightfall.

beats.title: Rama's Departure from the Citizens
beats.summary: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita spend a night on the banks of the Tamasa river. Rama decides to leave early to avoid the citizens who are following him. He instructs Sumantra to drive the chariot northward to mislead the people, then cross the river. They successfully depart without the citizens' knowledge.

beats.title: The Lament of Ayodhya's Citizens
beats.summary: The citizens of Ayodhya, grief-stricken after Rama's departure to the forest, return home and lament. They express their sorrow and devotion, praising Lakshmana and Sita for accompanying Rama, and declare that they have no use for home, wealth, or family without Rama. They also curse Kaikeyi for causing the exile.

beats.title: Rama's Departure from Ayodhya
beats.summary: In this chapter, the women of Ayodhya lament Rama's exile, cursing Kaikeyi. Rama, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana, departs from the city, crossing rivers and passing through villages, while hearing the people's criticism of King Dasharatha and Kaikeyi. He reflects on his past joys and continues his journey towards the forest.

beats.title: Rama's Arrival at the Ganges and Meeting with Guha
beats.summary: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita arrive at the Ganges near Shringaverapura. They meet their friend Guha, the king of the Nishadas, who offers hospitality. Rama declines all offerings except fodder for the horses, emphasizing his ascetic lifestyle. After performing evening rites, they rest under a tree, with Lakshmana keeping watch.

beats.title: The Night Vigil
beats.summary: Guha and Lakshmana keep vigil over the sleeping Rama and Sita. Guha offers his bed to Lakshmana, who refuses, expressing grief over Rama's exile and the impending death of King Dasharatha. Lakshmana laments the fate of Ayodhya and his family.

beats.title: Rama's Departure into Exile
beats.summary: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita prepare to cross the Ganga and enter the forest. Rama instructs the charioteer Sumantra to return to Ayodhya and comfort King Dasharatha. Sumantra laments the situation but obeys.

beats.title: Sumantra's Plea to Accompany Rama
beats.summary: Sumantra, the charioteer, pleads with Rama to allow him to accompany him into exile, expressing his devotion and the sorrow that Ayodhya will feel without Rama. Rama instructs Sumantra to convey messages to his mothers and Bharata, and to bring Bharata to the throne.

beats.title: Rama's Departure and Crossing of the Ganga
beats.summary: Rama, accompanied by Lakshmana and Sita, departs from Ayodhya into exile. He sends back the charioteer Sumantra to reassure Kaikeyi, takes leave of Guha, crosses the Ganga with his family, and Sita prays to the river for their safe return.

beats.title: Crossing the Ganga and First Night in Exile
beats.summary: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita cross the Ganga and enter the forest. Rama instructs Lakshmana to lead with Sita following, while he guards from behind. They hunt four deer for food and spend their first night under a tree. Rama reflects on the sorrows of exile, criticizes King Dasharatha's subservience to Kaikeyi's desires, and worries about his mothers' welfare.

beats.title: Departure to the Forest
beats.summary: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita depart from Ayodhya into the forest. Rama laments his mother Kaushalya's suffering and expresses fear that Kaikeyi may mistreat her. Lakshmana consoles Rama, vowing to follow him. They travel to the confluence of the Ganga and Yamuna (Prayaga).

beats.title: Arrival at Bharadvaja's Ashram and Direction to Chitrakuta
beats.summary: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita arrive at the confluence of the Ganga and Yamuna, near Bharadvaja's ashram. They meet the sage, who offers hospitality. Rama declines to stay near the ashram due to fear of crowds, so Bharadvaja directs them to Chitrakuta mountain, a peaceful and holy place suitable for their exile.

beats.title: Journey to Chitrakuta
beats.summary: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita, guided by sage Bharadvaja, cross the Yamuna river and proceed towards Chitrakuta mountain, where they spend a night and then continue their journey.

beats.title: Arrival at Chitrakuta
beats.summary: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita arrive at Chitrakuta mountain. Rama describes the beauty of the forest and instructs Lakshmana to build a hermitage. Lakshmana hunts a deer, cooks it, and Rama performs a ritual. They settle happily in the forest, leaving behind the sorrow of exile. Guha returns home after conversing with Sumantra, who goes back to Ayodhya.

beats.title: Sumantra's Return and Dasharatha's Grief
beats.summary: Sumantra returns to Ayodhya alone after leaving Rama at the Ganga. The city is grief-stricken. He reports to King Dasharatha, who faints upon hearing the news. Kausalya revives him and rebukes him for his silence, urging him to speak to the messenger.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Lament and Sumantra's Report
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, overwhelmed by grief after Rāma's exile, questions the charioteer Sumantra about Rāma's condition in the forest. Sumantra recounts Rāma's messages of respect and duty, and Lakṣmaṇa's angry outburst against the injustice.

beats.title: The Gathering at Kurukshetra and the Aftermath of Rama's Exile
beats.summary: King Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya what his sons and the Pandavas did on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. Sanjaya describes Duryodhana's speech to Drona, listing the warriors on both sides, the blowing of conches, and the emotional aftermath of Rama's exile in Ayodhya as recounted by the charioteer to King Dasharatha.

beats.title: Dasharatha's Grief and Kausalya's Lament
beats.summary: King Dasharatha, grieving the exile of Rama, laments and falls unconscious. Queen Kausalya also expresses her desire to join Rama. The charioteer Sumantra consoles her, describing Sita's resilience and Rama's virtuous conduct in the forest.

beats.title: Kaushalya's Lament
beats.summary: Kaushalya laments to King Dasharatha about the exile of Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana to the forest, expressing her grief and concern for their hardships, and criticizing the king's decision.

beats.title: Dasharatha's Grief and Kausalya's Consolation
beats.summary: King Dasharatha, overwhelmed by grief after Rama's exile, is consoled by Kausalya. He recalls a past sin (accidentally killing a young ascetic) and laments that sorrow destroys all virtues. The chapter ends with nightfall and the king falling asleep, tormented by guilt.

beats.title: Dasharatha's Confession: The Killing of Shravana
beats.summary: King Dasharatha recounts to Kausalya how he accidentally killed Shravana, a young ascetic, mistaking him for an animal while hunting at night. The dying boy laments his parents' fate and Dasharatha is cursed to die of grief for his son.

beats.title: The Accidental Killing of the Ascetic
beats.summary: Rama recounts to Sita how he accidentally killed a young ascetic while fetching water, mistaking him for an elephant. He then goes to the ascetic's blind parents and confesses, causing them great grief.

beats.title: Dasaratha's Accidental Killing of the Ascetic's Son
beats.summary: King Dasaratha recounts to his ministers how he accidentally killed a young ascetic, mistaking him for an elephant while hunting. The ascetic's blind parents curse him, but later forgive him after he leads them to their son's body. The father blesses Dasaratha, saying he will die of grief for his son, foreshadowing Rama's exile.

beats.title: Daśaratha's Grief and Death
beats.summary: King Daśaratha, grieving over Rāma's exile, recounts the story of a sage's son he accidentally killed, which led to a curse that he would die of separation from his son. He laments his fate and dies of grief. The next morning, the palace prepares for the daily rituals.

beats.title: The Death of Daśaratha
beats.summary: King Daśaratha dies, and his wives Kausalyā and Sumitrā lament. Kausalyā bitterly reproaches Kaikeyī for causing Rāma's exile and the king's death. The ministers place the king's body in an oil vat and await Rāma's return for the funeral rites.

beats.title: The Interregnum: Ayodhya Without a King
beats.summary: After King Dasharatha's death, Ayodhya falls into grief and chaos. The ministers and sages gather to decide on a new king, emphasizing the dangers of a kingless state.

beats.title: The Messengers Sent to Bharata
beats.summary: Vasishta sends messengers to bring Bharata back to Ayodhya after Dasaratha's death and Rama's exile. The messengers travel through various regions to reach Bharata in Rajagriha.

beats.title: Bharata's Dream and the Arrival of Messengers
beats.summary: Bharata recounts a disturbing dream to his friends, interpreting it as an omen of death for himself, Rama, or Lakshmana. Messengers then arrive from Ayodhya urging him to return quickly due to an urgent matter.

beats.title: Bharata's Departure from His Maternal Grandfather's House
beats.summary: Bharata, after inquiring about the well-being of Rama, Lakshmana, Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi, takes leave of his maternal grandfather and uncle, receives gifts, and departs with Shatrughna for Ayodhya. He crosses several rivers and mountains, resting at the Yamuna.
